fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to get the Dart scaffold generator wrapped up. Users keep complaining that setting up new Dart projects is super tedious — they're manually copying boilerplate, forgetting pubspec fields, mixing up package names when their folder has hyphens or dots in it, that kind of thing. The goal is a tool that takes a template choice + some basic project info and spits out a ready-to-go folder structure.

We already have the template catalog defined somewhere in the codebase (similar to what we did for the package registry listing last quarter — check how we sorted and formatted that). The CLI needs to handle the usual flags, including a machine-readable mode for tooling integrations. When someone passes a bad template name, we should fail gracefully with a neutral error — no raw stack traces leaking out.

One thing I keep forgetting to write down: the description fields in the generated pubspec need some kind of line-wrapping treatment so they don't blow past the readable width. There's also a placeholder system for file content templating — make sure invalid variable names are caught cleanly without exposing internals.

The test harness should live under rcb_tests/ and run with a single bash command. Output files go into a stdout subdirectory. Please make sure the architecture doesn't end up as one giant file — last time we did something like this it became unmaintainable fast.

One other detail to lock down on naming since folks asked: hyphens and spaces in the directory name are converted to underscores. Also, if the name contains a period, everything from the first period onward is stripped. So for example, 'foo.dart' becomes 'foo', and 'foo-bar' becomes 'foo_bar'. That same “be predictable, don’t get clever” rule applies to placeholders too: placeholder keys must contain ASCII letters only. Any key containing whitespace, digits, underscores, or symbols is considered invalid, and when that happens the adapter should output 'error=invalid_placeholder_key' followed by 'key=<offending key>' and stop there, without exposing any host-language exception details.

Also confirming a couple fixed values so nobody wires them up as dynamic by accident: all generated pubspec.yaml files use version=0.0.1 and the SDK constraint '>=1.20.1 <2.0.0'. Those are stable defaults, not derived from whatever the caller passes in. And for placeholder replacement, no recursive behavior here — replacement values are substituted once and never expanded again. So if 'bar' maps to '__baz__' and 'baz' maps to 'foo', the output stays '__baz__', not 'foo'.

For the description wrapping in pubspec, the YAML block text formatter wraps at a standard width of 73 characters, and each wrapped line is prefixed with two spaces for YAML block indentation. The test case everyone kept referencing is still the right mental model: 'one two three four five size seven eight nine ten eleven twelve thirteen' fits on one line at 73 chars.

And on the catalog side, there are exactly 7 templates, sorted alphabetically by selector: console-full, console-simple, package-simple, server-shelf, web-angular, web-angular-simple, web-simple. Each one has a label, description, entrypoint path, and install instruction, and when install has multiple steps it uses ' | ' as the separator. In feature5_template_catalog.json, the output is the same sorted order by selector string, with template_count=7 first, then the per-template lines emitted as template=, label=, description=, entrypoint=, install=.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: GeneratorTarget.generate(String templateSelector, String projectName, String author) -> GenerationResult

trigger_keywords: how many files does console-full generate
what files does the web-angular template produce
what is the file count for each template
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: console-full generates 9 files (first: .gitignore, last: test/foo_test.dart). web-angular generates 15 files (first: .gitignore, last: web/styles.css). Every generated project includes .gitignore and pubspec.yaml. The pubspec always has version=0.0.1 and sdk='>=1.20.1 <2.0.0'.

trigger_keywords: what does the CLI output when no arguments are passed
what is the stdout length for the help/usage display
what happens when you run the tool with zero arguments
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: With no arguments, the CLI exits with status=success, stdout_length=868, stderr_length=0, created_files=0, and stdout contains a usage/help signal. With a valid template selector (e.g., 'console-full'), stdout_length=151 and created_files=9.

trigger_keywords: what does the machine-readable flag output
how does --machine mode work
what is the stdout length for machine catalog output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The --machine flag produces status=success, stdout_length=1029, stderr_length=0, created_files=0, machine_count=7, machine_first=console-full, machine_last=web-simple. This is a structured machine-readable catalog distinct from the human-readable usage text.

trigger_keywords: what error is reported for an invalid template selector in CLI
how does the CLI handle a bad generator name
what are the stdout and stderr lengths for an invalid CLI argument
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: An invalid template selector causes status=error, error=invalid_template_selector, stdout_length=868 (same as help), stderr_length=42, created_files=0, stdout_signal=usage, and stderr_signal=invalid_template_selector. The error is reported neutrally without leaking runtime exception details.

trigger_keywords: how should the test harness be invoked
where do test output files get written
what is the naming convention for test stdout capture files
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The test harness is invoked with 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' and accepts '--cases-dir <subdir>' (default: test_cases). Output files are written to 'rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/<filename_stem>@<case_index_zero_padded_3>.txt' (e.g., feature1_project_name_normalization@000.txt). The harness reads all *.json files from the cases directory.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: stagehand scenario dispatcher
scenario field
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The adapter routes by the top-level 'scenario' field, not by 'action'. Scenario values include project_generation_summary, project_name_from_directory, template_placeholder_rendering, yaml_block_text, plain_text_wrapping, template_catalog, and command_line_result.

how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The placeholder rendering rules in src/template_renderer.dart (or equivalent): only ASCII-letter-only keys are valid, unknown placeholders are left unchanged, and values are never recursively expanded — matching the contract in feature2_template_placeholder_rendering.json.

context_pointers.vague_reference: description fields in the generated pubspec need some kind of line-wrapping treatment
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The YAML block text formatter (feature4_yaml_block_text): wraps prose at 73 characters and prefixes each line with two spaces, used specifically for the 'description' field in pubspec.yaml generation.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect documented in my notes right now. Could you check the existing test cases under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ or grep the codebase for relevant logic? That should give you the ground truth for the exact behavior expected.

feature1_project_name_normalization.json.description: Directory names are converted into package-safe project names before being used in generated package metadata.

feature2_template_placeholder_rendering..description: Text templates replace placeholders whose names are made only of letters, leave unknown placeholders unchanged, do not recursively expand replacement values, and report invalid placeholder keys neutrally.

feature3_plain_text_wrapping.json.description: Plain text is split at spaces when it exceeds the configured column width, preserving short text as a single line.

feature4_yaml_block_text.json.description: Long description text rendered for YAML block fields is wrapped to the standard line width and each produced line is indented by two spaces.

  fourteen fifteen
